title: Wireless Link Budget Guide
summary: Understand frequency, distance, transmit power, antenna gain, extra losses, receiver sensitivity, and wireless fade margin.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether a wireless link has enough signal margin after distance, frequency, antenna gain, other losses, and receiver sensitivity are considered.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document wireless design assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch RF, capacity, or margin pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning outdoor links, building-to-building links, directional antennas, or any RF path where distance and path loss matter.
bullets: Early wireless design planning.
Expansion review or refresh planning.
Existing-system cleanup, validation, or troubleshooting support.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical wireless planning terms.

items.label: Frequency (GHz)
items.description: The operating frequency of the wireless link.
items.whyItMatters: Frequency changes path loss and affects how much margin remains over distance.
items.commonMistake: Comparing links at different frequencies without recalculating path loss.

items.label: Distance (ft)
items.description: The path distance between transmitter and receiver.
items.whyItMatters: Distance is a major driver of free-space path loss.
items.commonMistake: Using map distance without considering real path, mounting, or line of sight.

links.label: Plan PtP distance and SNR together

items.label: TX Power (dBm)
items.description: The transmitter output power before antenna gain and losses are applied.
items.whyItMatters: TX power helps determine received signal, but legal limits and antenna gain still matter.
items.commonMistake: Turning up transmit power without checking EIRP or client/link balance.

items.label: TX Antenna Gain (dBi)
items.description: The gain of the transmitting antenna in the intended direction.
items.whyItMatters: Antenna gain focuses energy and improves the link in the aimed direction.
items.commonMistake: Adding gain without accounting for aiming, beamwidth, or regulatory EIRP.

items.label: RX Antenna Gain (dBi)
items.description: The gain of the receiving antenna in the direction of the transmitter.
items.whyItMatters: Receive gain improves link margin when antennas are aligned and installed correctly.
items.commonMistake: Assuming antenna gain helps if the antenna pattern is misaligned.

items.label: Other Losses (dB)
items.description: Additional losses from cables, connectors, obstruction, foliage, weather, or installation conditions.
items.whyItMatters: Small extra losses can consume fade margin quickly.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring cable and connector loss in the final link budget.

items.label: Receiver Sensitivity (dBm)
items.description: The minimum signal level the receiver needs for the target modulation or data rate.
items.whyItMatters: Receiver sensitivity sets the threshold for whether the link has enough signal to operate reliably.
items.commonMistake: Using the most sensitive low-rate number while expecting high throughput.

links.label: Compare received signal to noise margin

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the link appears to have practical margin. Watch means antenna, distance, loss, or sensitivity assumptions should be reviewed. Risk means the link may be unreliable or unable to support the expected rate.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but RF conditions, client behavior, or operating margin should be reviewed.
Risk: add capacity, reduce assumptions, improve placement, or validate with a survey before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Wireless planning problems usually come from treating Wi-Fi like a fixed cable instead of a shared RF environment affected by airtime, interference, device capability, channel reuse, and roaming behavior.
bullets: Sizing only for coverage and ignoring capacity.
Using ideal vendor rates as if they are sustained usable throughput.
Ignoring noise floor, channel overlap, client mix, and airtime contention.
Failing to document assumptions before AP count, channel plan, or antenna placement is finalized.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace a wireless survey, manufacturer documentation, controller validation, spectrum analysis, or project-specific RF engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications and controller guidance still matter.
Client device capability can be the limiting factor even when AP hardware is strong.
Walls, mounting height, interference, antenna pattern, and channel reuse can change the final result.
A wireless design with margin is usually easier to support, tune, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal wireless design review, site survey validation, manufacturer guidance, or project-specific responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual AP model, antenna pattern, client mix, controller settings, and site survey results.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for coverage, speed, roaming, and reliability.
Document assumptions before implementation or final AP placement.
